God speaks the heavens and earth into existence in six days and rests on the seventh. He forms Adam from the dust, plants a garden in Eden, and makes Eve from Adam's rib as his help meet.
The Line of Cain; Lamech's Boast

The serpent deceives Eve, Adam eats, and sin enters the world — but God promises a Redeemer even as He pronounces the curse. Cain murders his brother Abel out of jealousy, and his line grows in violence under Lamech.
Sons of God and Daughters of Men
Enoch walks with God and is taken without seeing death. As mankind multiplies, wickedness fills the earth until God determines to send judgment — but Noah finds grace in His eyes.
God commands Noah to build an ark, and the flood destroys every living thing outside it. After the waters recede, Noah offers a sacrifice and God smells a sweet savour.
God sets His rainbow in the cloud as a covenant never to flood the earth again. Noah's drunkenness brings a curse on Canaan, and at Babel God confounds man's proud language and scatters the nations.

God calls Abram out of his country with the promise of a great nation. In Egypt Abram falters and calls Sarai his sister, then back in Canaan he graciously lets Lot choose the well-watered plain toward Sodom.
Hagar and the Birth of Ishmael

Abram rescues Lot from four kings and is blessed by Melchizedek, priest of the most high God. God promises Abram descendants as the stars, and he believes — counted to him for righteousness — but Sarai's plan with Hagar produces Ishmael.
God seals His covenant with circumcision and renames Abram and Sarai. Three visitors promise a son within the year — Sarah laughs — and Abraham pleads with God to spare Sodom for even ten righteous.
The Destruction of Sodom; Lot's Wife

Fire and brimstone fall on Sodom and Gomorrah; Lot's wife looks back and becomes a pillar of salt. Lot's daughters bear Moab and Ammon in shame, and Abraham again misrepresents Sarah, this time to Abimelech.
The Birth of Isaac; Hagar and Ishmael Cast Out
Isaac, the child of promise, is born, and Hagar and Ishmael are sent away — yet God preserves them. On Mount Moriah Abraham raises the knife over his son, and God provides a ram in Isaac's stead.
The Death and Burial of Sarah
Abraham buys the cave of Machpelah to bury Sarah. His servant, led by answered prayer, finds Rebekah at the well as a bride for Isaac, and Abraham dies old and full of years.
Jacob and Esau; the Birthright Sold
Esau despises his birthright and sells it for a bowl of pottage. Isaac re-digs his father's wells in peace, but Jacob, coached by Rebekah, deceives his blind father and steals Esau's blessing.
Fleeing Esau, Jacob dreams of a ladder to heaven at Bethel, where God confirms the covenant. At Haran he serves Laban seven years for Rachel — and wakes married to Leah, then serves seven more.
Through Leah, Rachel, and their handmaids, eleven sons and a daughter are born to Jacob. Despite Laban's scheming, God multiplies Jacob's flocks until he grows exceedingly wealthy.
Jacob Wrestles with God at Peniel
Jacob slips away from Laban, who pursues but parts with a covenant. At Peniel Jacob wrestles with God until daybreak and is renamed Israel, then meets Esau — who runs to embrace him.
Return to Bethel; Deaths of Rachel and Isaac
Joseph's Dreams and the Coat of Many Colours

Simeon and Levi avenge their sister Dinah with the sword at Shechem. Jacob returns to Bethel where God renews the covenant; Rachel dies bearing Benjamin. Joseph's coat and dreams stir his brothers' hatred.

Joseph's brothers sell him to Ishmaelite traders and dip his coat in blood. Judah's sin with Tamar brings forth Pharez, and in Egypt Joseph flees Potiphar's wife — and lands in prison for his integrity.
The Brothers' First Journey to Egypt

Joseph interprets the dreams of Pharaoh's butler and baker in prison. Two years later he interprets Pharaoh's dreams of famine and is exalted over Egypt — and his brothers come bowing to buy corn, not knowing him.

The brothers return with Benjamin, and Joseph tests them with the silver cup. Judah offers himself in Benjamin's place — and Joseph, weeping aloud, reveals himself: God sent me before you to preserve life.
Jacob and all his house come down to Egypt, and father and son are reunited in Goshen. Joseph wisely administers the famine years, gathering all Egypt's money, cattle, and land for Pharaoh.
Jacob Blesses His Sons; His Death
The Burial of Jacob; the Death of Joseph

Jacob crosses his hands to bless Ephraim above Manasseh, then prophesies over all twelve sons — the sceptre shall not depart from Judah. Joseph forgives his brothers fully: ye thought evil against me; but God meant it unto good.
A new Pharaoh enslaves Israel and orders the sons drowned, but the midwives fear God. Baby Moses is drawn from the Nile by Pharaoh's own daughter; grown, he kills an Egyptian and flees to Midian.
God speaks from the burning bush — I AM THAT I AM — and commissions the reluctant Moses to deliver Israel. With Aaron as his spokesman and signs in hand, Moses returns to Egypt.
Pharaoh answers Moses' demand by forcing Israel to make bricks without straw. God renews His covenant promise, and Aaron's rod becomes a serpent that swallows the magicians' rods.
The Nile turns to blood, frogs cover the land, and the dust becomes lice. Even the magicians confess 'this is the finger of God,' but Pharaoh's heart is hardened.

Swarms of flies fill Egypt — but not Goshen, where God puts a division between His people and Pharaoh's. The cattle die of murrain and boils break out on man and beast, yet Pharaoh will not let Israel go.
Fire mingled with hail flattens Egypt's crops, locusts devour what remains, and thick darkness that may be felt covers the land three days — while all Israel has light in their dwellings.
Death of the Firstborn; the Exodus

God announces the death of Egypt's firstborn and institutes the Passover: a lamb slain, its blood on the doorposts — when I see the blood, I will pass over you. At midnight the destroyer strikes, and Israel marches out of Egypt.
The Pillar of Cloud and Fire
The Song of Moses and Miriam
God leads Israel by a pillar of cloud by day and fire by night. The Red Sea parts before them and closes over Pharaoh's army, and Moses and Miriam lead Israel in a song of triumph.
Bitter Water Made Sweet at Marah
Water from the Rock; Victory over Amalek

At Marah a tree cast into bitter waters makes them sweet. God rains manna from heaven and sends quail, brings water from the smitten rock at Horeb, and gives victory over Amalek while Moses' hands are held up.
Jethro counsels Moses to appoint able men as judges over Israel. At Sinai the mountain quakes with fire and smoke as God descends, and He speaks the Ten Commandments to His people.
The Covenant Confirmed; Moses on the Mount

The covenant is sealed with blood and Moses ascends into the cloud forty days. Below, Aaron fashions a golden calf and the people corrupt themselves; Moses intercedes for them and pleads, shew me thy glory.
The Tables Renewed; Moses' Shining Face
God rewrites the tables of stone and proclaims His name — merciful and gracious, longsuffering. Moses comes down from the mount unaware that the skin of his face shines.
The Tabernacle Built and Filled with Glory
Willing-hearted Israel gives and builds until the tabernacle stands complete, exactly as God commanded. Then the cloud covers the tent, and the glory of the LORD fills the tabernacle.
The Sin of Nadab and Abihu
Fire at Taberah; Quail and Plague at Kibroth-hattaavah
Nadab and Abihu offer strange fire and are consumed before the LORD. A blasphemer is stoned outside the camp, and Israel's lusting for meat brings quail — and a plague — at Kibroth-hattaavah.
Miriam and Aaron Oppose Moses; Miriam's Leprosy

Miriam is struck with leprosy for speaking against Moses, then healed at his prayer. Ten spies bring an evil report of Canaan; only Caleb and Joshua believe God — and unbelieving Israel is sentenced to forty years in the wilderness.

A man gathering sticks on the sabbath is stoned. The earth opens and swallows Korah and his rebels, fire consumes the two hundred fifty, and Aaron's rod buds, blossoms, and yields almonds to settle the priesthood.
Edom Refuses Passage; the Death of Aaron

At Meribah Moses smites the rock in anger instead of speaking to it, and is barred from the promised land. Aaron dies on Mount Hor, and when fiery serpents bite the people, all who look to the brasen serpent live.
Israel defeats Sihon king of the Amorites and Og king of Bashan. Balak hires Balaam to curse Israel, but the prophet's own donkey sees the angel of the LORD and speaks to stop his madness.
Balaam opens his mouth to curse but can only bless — a Star shall come out of Jacob. Yet Israel falls into whoredom at Baal-peor, and Phinehas's zeal with a javelin stays the plague.
The daughters of Zelophehad win their father's inheritance. Moses lays hands on Joshua as his successor, and Israel executes God's vengeance on Midian — Balaam falls with them.
Reuben and Gad Settle East of Jordan
Moses Pleads to Enter the Land
Moses Views the Land; the Death of Moses

Reuben and Gad receive their inheritance east of Jordan on the promise to fight with their brethren. Moses pleads to cross over but is denied; from Pisgah he views the land, dies in Moab, and God Himself buries him.
God commissions Joshua: be strong and of a good courage, for the LORD thy God is with thee. In Jericho the harlot Rahab hides the spies and hangs the scarlet line in her window.
Crossing the Jordan; the Memorial Stones
Circumcision at Gilgal; the Captain of the LORD's Host

The Jordan stands in a heap as the priests bear the ark across, and twelve stones memorialize the crossing. At Gilgal the new generation is circumcised, and Joshua meets the Captain of the LORD's host.
Jericho's walls fall flat at the shout on the seventh day, and Rahab is spared. Achan's hidden wedge of gold brings defeat at Ai until his sin is judged; then Ai falls by ambush.
The Covenant Renewed at Mount Ebal
The Sun Stands Still; the Southern Campaign

Joshua reads the whole law before Israel at Mount Ebal. The Gibeonites deceive Israel with mouldy bread into a league, and in the battle that follows, Joshua commands the sun to stand still — and it obeys.

Joshua conquers the northern kings and takes the whole land. Eighty-five-year-old Caleb claims Hebron — give me this mountain — and the eastern tribes' altar of witness nearly sparks civil war until its purpose is explained.
Joshua charges Israel: choose you this day whom ye will serve; but as for me and my house, we will serve the LORD. After his death the tribes fail to drive out the Canaanites fully.
A generation arises that knows not the LORD, and the cycle begins — sin, servitude, supplication, salvation. Othniel delivers Israel, and left-handed Ehud plunges his dagger into fat king Eglon.
Shamgar slays six hundred Philistines with an ox goad. Deborah calls Barak to battle against Sisera, whom Jael fells with a tent nail, and Israel sings the victory.
The Call of Gideon; the Fleece

The angel calls Gideon, a thresher hiding from Midian, and confirms his call by the fleece. God whittles his army to three hundred, who rout Midian with trumpets and lamps — but Gideon's golden ephod becomes a snare.
Abimelech murders his seventy brothers to be king, and Jotham's parable of the bramble curses him — fulfilled when a millstone crushes his skull. Tola and Jair judge Israel in quieter years.
The outcast Jephthah delivers Israel from Ammon but makes a rash vow concerning his only daughter. At the Jordan fords, the password Shibboleth exposes forty-two thousand Ephraimites.

Three minor judges pass, then the angel announces to Manoah's barren wife a son who will be a Nazarite from the womb. Young Samson kills a lion barehanded and poses the riddle of honey from its carcass.

Samson burns Philistine fields with three hundred foxes and slays a thousand men with the jawbone of an ass. Delilah coaxes out his secret, his eyes are put out — but in death at Dagon's temple he slays more than in his life. Micah sets up a house of idols.
The tribe of Dan steals Micah's idols and his priest. The horror at Gibeah against the Levite's concubine plunges Israel into civil war, and Benjamin is nearly wiped out.
Naomi and Ruth Return to Bethlehem
Ruth Gleans in the Field of Boaz
Israel finds wives for Benjamin's six hundred survivors. Widowed Ruth clings to Naomi — thy people shall be my people, and thy God my God — and gleans in the field of Boaz, who shows her grace.
Hannah's Prayer; the Birth of Samuel

Ruth lies at Boaz's feet, and he pledges to play the kinsman's part. Boaz redeems her at the gate, and their son Obed becomes grandfather to David. Barren Hannah pours out her soul, and God gives her Samuel.
Hannah's Song; the Sons of Eli

Hannah's song exalts the God who raises the poor from the dust, while Eli's vile sons profane the offerings. God calls the child Samuel by name in the night, and later the ark is captured — Ichabod, the glory is departed.
Ebenezer: "Hitherto Hath the LORD Helped Us"

In Philistia the idol Dagon falls broken before the ark, and plagues drive the Philistines to send it home on a new cart. At Mizpeh Israel repents, God thunders on their enemies, and Samuel raises the stone Ebenezer.

Israel demands a king like all the nations, rejecting God's rule, and He grants their request with warning. Samuel anoints tall Saul, out searching for his father's donkeys, and he is proclaimed king at Mizpeh.

Saul rallies Israel and rescues Jabesh-gilead from the Ammonites. Samuel delivers his farewell — God sends thunder in wheat harvest as a sign — and Saul, impatient at Gilgal, offers the sacrifice himself and forfeits his dynasty.
Jonathan's Daring Raid; Saul's Rash Oath
Saul Rejected; "To Obey Is Better Than Sacrifice"
David Anointed; David Plays for Saul

Jonathan and his armourbearer rout a Philistine garrison alone, but Saul's rash oath nearly costs his son's life. Saul spares Agag and the best of the spoil, and Samuel declares him rejected — to obey is better than sacrifice. God sends Samuel to Bethlehem to anoint the shepherd boy David.

David fells the giant Goliath with a sling and a stone in the name of the LORD of hosts. Jonathan's soul knits to David's, but the women's song — Saul his thousands, David his ten thousands — kindles Saul's jealousy, and Michal lets David down through a window.
The Shewbread; David Feigns Madness at Gath
The Cave of Adullam; the Priests of Nob Slain

Jonathan's arrows signal David that Saul means murder, and the friends part weeping. David eats the shewbread at Nob and feigns madness before the king of Gath; Saul answers by slaughtering the priests of Nob.
David Saves Keilah; Hunted in Ziph
David Spares Saul in the Cave

David rescues Keilah, then flees Saul through the wilderness of Ziph. In the cave at En-gedi David cuts Saul's skirt but will not touch the LORD's anointed, and wise Abigail turns David from avenging himself on churlish Nabal.
David Spares Saul Again: the Spear and the Cruse
David at Ziklag Among the Philistines
Saul and the Witch of Endor

David steals Saul's spear and water cruse from beside his sleeping head, sparing him a second time. He takes refuge in Ziklag among the Philistines, while desperate Saul consults the witch of Endor and hears his doom.
The Death of Saul and Jonathan

The Philistine lords refuse to let David march with them. He returns to find Ziklag burned and encourages himself in the LORD, recovering all from the Amalekites — while on Mount Gilboa, Saul and Jonathan fall.
David Hears of Saul's Death; the Lament
David King over Judah; Civil War Begins
David laments Saul and Jonathan — how are the mighty fallen. Anointed king over Judah at Hebron, he faces long war with Saul's house until Abner defects, only to be murdered by Joab.
David King over All Israel; Jerusalem Taken
The Ark Brought to Jerusalem; Uzzah; Michal

Ish-bosheth's murderers bring his head to David and receive death for their treachery. David is crowned over all Israel and takes Zion; bringing up the ark, Uzzah dies for touching it, and David dances before the LORD while Michal despises him.
God covenants with David: thy throne shall be established for ever. David subdues his enemies on every side and seeks out lame Mephibosheth, Jonathan's son, to eat at the king's table continually.

David's kindness to Ammon is repaid with insult and war. At home in the season of battle, David takes Bath-sheba and arranges Uriah's death — and Nathan's parable of the ewe lamb pierces him: thou art the man.
The Woman of Tekoah; Absalom Recalled

The sword rises in David's own house: Amnon violates Tamar, and Absalom kills him. Recalled through the woman of Tekoah's tale, Absalom steals the hearts of Israel and David flees Jerusalem weeping up Olivet.
Shimei curses the fleeing king, and David bears it. Hushai's counsel defeats Ahithophel's, and in the wood of Ephraim Absalom hangs by his hair from an oak — David mourns: O Absalom, my son, my son.
The Gibeonites Avenged; Battles with the Giants
David returns over Jordan, showing grace to Shimei, Mephibosheth, and aged Barzillai. Sheba's revolt ends with his head over a wall, and David's mighty men fell the last sons of the giant.
David's Census; the Plague; Araunah's Threshing Floor

The exploits of David's mighty men are recorded — and the water of Bethlehem he would not drink. His prideful census brings plague, halted at Araunah's threshing floor, and as Adonijah grasps at the crown, Solomon is anointed king.
Solomon Asks for Wisdom; the Two Mothers

Dying David charges Solomon to walk in God's ways, and the kingdom is settled through swift justice. At Gibeon Solomon asks for an understanding heart, and his verdict between two mothers over one living baby reveals God's wisdom in him.

With cedar from Hiram of Tyre and stone made ready before it was brought, Solomon builds the house of the LORD in seven years — no hammer heard in the building — with its pillars, courts, and vessels of gold.
The Temple Dedicated; Glory Fills the House
God Appears to Solomon the Second Time

At the dedication the cloud fills the temple so the priests cannot stand to minister, and Solomon prays with hands spread toward heaven. God appears again with promise and warning, and the queen of Sheba confesses the half was not told me.
The Kingdom Divided; Jeroboam's Golden Calves
The Man of God from Judah and the Old Prophet

Solomon's strange wives turn his heart after other gods, and adversaries rise. Rehoboam's harsh answer splits the kingdom, Jeroboam sets up golden calves at Bethel and Dan, and the disobedient man of God from Judah is slain by a lion.
Ahijah's Prophecy; the Death of Jeroboam's Son
Elijah, the Ravens, and the Widow of Zarephath

Ahijah pronounces doom on Jeroboam's house. Elijah declares drought, is fed by ravens at Cherith and by the widow's unfailing barrel at Zarephath — then on Carmel, fire falls from heaven and the people cry, the LORD, he is the God.
Elijah Flees; the Still Small Voice; Elisha Called

Elijah flees Jezebel to Horeb, where God speaks not in wind, earthquake, or fire but a still small voice, and Elisha is called from the plough. Ahab twice defeats Ben-hadad but spares him, and Jezebel murders Naboth for his vineyard.
Micaiah's Prophecy; the Death of Ahab
Fire from Heaven on Ahaziah's Captains
Elijah Taken Up; Elisha Takes the Mantle

Micaiah alone tells Ahab the truth, and a bow drawn at a venture finds the king between the joints of his harness. Fire from heaven consumes Ahaziah's captains, and Elijah is carried up by a whirlwind in a chariot of fire — his mantle falls to Elisha.
The Waters Healed; the Mocking Youths
Moab Rebels; Water Fills the Valley

Elisha heals Jericho's waters with salt, and bears judge the mockers of Bethel. Water miraculously fills the valley to save three kings' armies, and a widow's pot of oil fills every borrowed vessel to pay her debt.
Death in the Pot; a Hundred Men Fed

The Shunammite's promised son dies and is raised through Elisha's prayer. The poisoned pottage is healed with meal and a hundred men fed with twenty loaves; Naaman the Syrian dips seven times in Jordan and is clean — but greedy Gehazi inherits his leprosy.
Chariots of Fire; the Syrians Blinded

Elisha makes a borrowed axe head swim. When Syria surrounds Dothan, the servant's eyes are opened to see the mountain full of horses and chariots of fire — they that be with us are more than they that be with them.
The Siege of Samaria; the Four Lepers

Famine grips besieged Samaria until four lepers find the Syrian camp abandoned — a day of good tidings. The doubting lord is trodden in the gate as Elisha foretold; the Shunammite's land is restored, and Elisha weeps over what Hazael will do.
Jehu Anointed; the Deaths of Joram and Jezebel
Jehu Destroys the House of Ahab and Baal Worship
Athaliah's Usurpation; Joash Hidden and Crowned

Jehu is anointed to cut off Ahab's house; he drives furiously, slays Joram, and Jezebel is thrown down to the dogs. He purges Baal from Israel, while in Judah wicked Athaliah usurps the throne — but baby Joash is hidden six years and crowned.
The Death of Elisha; His Bones Revive a Man
Amaziah Challenges Jehoash: the Thistle and the Cedar

Joash collects offerings in a bored chest and repairs the LORD's house. Dying Elisha has king Joash smite arrows on the ground; later a dead man revives at the touch of Elisha's bones. Proud Amaziah ignores the parable of the thistle and is routed.
Ahaz Copies the Altar of Damascus

Ahaz replaces the LORD's altar with a copy of the pagan altar at Damascus. For centuries of idolatry and rejected prophets, the northern kingdom falls — Assyria carries Israel away and resettles Samaria with foreigners.
Hezekiah's Illness; the Shadow Turns Back

Hezekiah spreads Sennacherib's blasphemous letter before the LORD, and the angel of the LORD smites 185,000 Assyrians in a night. Sick unto death, Hezekiah weeps and receives fifteen more years, confirmed by the shadow going back ten degrees.
Josiah Finds the Book of the Law

Manasseh fills Jerusalem with innocent blood and builds altars to all the host of heaven. Young Josiah rends his clothes when the lost book of the law is read, purges the land of idols, and keeps a Passover like none before him.
The Fall of Jerusalem; Judah Carried Captive

Nebuchadnezzar besieges Jerusalem, burns the temple, breaks down the walls, and carries Judah to Babylon. Amid the chronicles stands Jabez, who called on the God of Israel — and God granted him that which he requested.
David Prepares Materials for the Temple
David's Charge to Solomon; the Freewill Offering

Though barred from building, David prepares gold, silver, brass, and cedar in abundance for the temple. He charges Solomon before all Israel and leads a freewill offering — all things come of thee, and of thine own have we given thee.
Asa's Victory over Zerah the Ethiopian

Abijah defeats double numbers because Judah relied upon the LORD. Asa cries to God against a million Ethiopians and prevails, and Jehoshaphat sends the singers before the army — the battle is not yours, but God's.

King Uzziah, strong and famous, presumes to burn incense and is struck with leprosy at the altar. Hezekiah's great Passover brings joy unknown since Solomon, and Manasseh — dragged to Babylon in fetters — humbles himself, and God is intreated of him.
The Decree of Cyrus; the Return
The Altar Rebuilt; the Foundation Laid

God stirs Cyrus king of Persia to proclaim the return, and nearly fifty thousand go up to Jerusalem. The altar is rebuilt and the temple foundation laid — the young shout for joy while the old men who saw the first house weep aloud.
The Work Resumes; Darius Confirms the Decree

Adversaries weary the builders and a royal decree stops the work for years. Stirred by Haggai and Zechariah the work resumes, Darius finds Cyrus's decree, and the house is finished and dedicated with joy.

Ezra the ready scribe, the good hand of his God upon him, leads a second return. He proclaims a fast at the river Ahava rather than ask the king for soldiers, and God brings the company and treasure safely to Jerusalem.
Nehemiah Weeps and Prays for Jerusalem

Ezra rends his garment and prays in shame over Israel's mixed marriages, and the people covenant to put things right. In Shushan, Nehemiah hears of Jerusalem's broken walls and weeps, fasts, and prays before the God of heaven.
Before the King; the Night Inspection

The king grants sad-faced Nehemiah leave and letters, and by night he surveys the ruined walls. The people build with a mind to work — sword in one hand, trowel in the other — despite Sanballat's mockery and plots.
Nehemiah Stops the Oppression of the Poor
Plots Against Nehemiah; the Wall Finished in 52 Days
Ezra Reads the Law; the Feast of Tabernacles

Nehemiah rebukes the nobles for usury against their brethren. Refusing every snare — should such a man as I flee? — he finishes the wall in fifty-two days, and Ezra reads the law from morning to midday: the joy of the LORD is your strength.

Israel confesses generations of sin as the Levites rehearse God's longsuffering mercies, and the people seal a covenant. Two great companies of singers walk the wall at its dedication, and the joy of Jerusalem is heard afar off.
Esther Made Queen; Mordecai Uncovers a Plot

Returning, Nehemiah purges the temple chambers, restores the tithes, and contends for the sabbath. In Persia, queen Vashti is deposed, the orphan Esther is crowned in her place, and Mordecai uncovers a plot against the king.

Haman, enraged that Mordecai will not bow, buys a decree to destroy all Jews. Mordecai charges Esther — who knoweth whether thou art come to the kingdom for such a time as this? — and she risks death to approach the king, while Haman builds a gallows fifty cubits high.
Haman Hanged on His Own Gallows

On a sleepless night the king hears how Mordecai saved him, and Haman must lead his enemy through the streets in royal honour. At Esther's second banquet the plot is exposed, and Haman hangs on his own gallows.
The New Decree; the Jews Delivered; Purim
Since a Persian decree cannot be reversed, a new one arms the Jews to defend themselves, and their sorrow is turned to joy. The feast of Purim is established to remember the deliverance.
Job's Testing: Satan Before the LORD
Satan claims Job serves God only for gain, and in a day Job loses children, wealth, and health — yet he worships: the LORD gave, and the LORD hath taken away; blessed be the name of the LORD.
God Answers out of the Whirlwind; Job Restored

God answers Job out of the whirlwind with questions no man can answer — where wast thou when I laid the foundations of the earth? Job abhors himself and repents in dust and ashes, and God turns his captivity, giving him double.
Isaiah's Vision and Call: "Here Am I; Send Me"
Isaiah's Three-Year Sign: Naked and Barefoot

In the year king Uzziah died, Isaiah sees the LORD high and lifted up, is cleansed by a live coal, and answers: here am I; send me. He walks stripped and barefoot three years as a sign against Egypt and Ethiopia.

Isaiah's account of the great deliverance: Rabshakeh's taunts, Hezekiah's prayer, and the angel's overthrow of Assyria. Hezekiah is healed as the sundial's shadow returns — but showing his treasures to Babylon's envoys plants the seed of exile.

God ordains Jeremiah a prophet before his birth and touches his mouth. A marred linen girdle pictures ruined Judah's pride, and at the potter's house the clay marred in his hand teaches God's sovereign right over nations.
The Broken Bottle in the Valley of Hinnom
Jeremiah's Trial After the Temple Sermon

Jeremiah shatters a potter's bottle in Hinnom: so will I break this people. His temple sermon nearly costs his life, and the false prophet Hananiah breaks Jeremiah's wooden yoke — and dies that year as the LORD said.
Jeremiah Buys the Field at Anathoth

With Babylon at the gates, Jeremiah buys a field at Anathoth — houses and fields shall be possessed again in this land. The Rechabites' obedience to their father shames Judah, and king Jehoiakim slices the prophet's scroll with a penknife and burns it.
Jeremiah in the Dungeon; Ebed-melech's Rescue

Jeremiah is beaten, imprisoned, and lowered into a miry dungeon for preaching surrender to Babylon. Ebed-melech the Ethiopian draws him up with old rags and cords, and the prophet counsels wavering Zedekiah in secret.
The Fall of Jerusalem; Gedaliah; Flight to Egypt

The wall is broken, Zedekiah's sons are slain before his eyes, and Jerusalem burns. Gedaliah the governor is assassinated, and the remnant — against Jeremiah's warning — flees to Egypt, dragging the prophet along.
Ezekiel's Vision of the Wheels; His Call

By the river Chebar Ezekiel sees the living creatures and wheels within wheels — the likeness of the glory of the LORD. He eats the scroll, sweet as honey, and is made a watchman to the house of Israel.
Ezekiel's Sign-Acts: the Tile, the Siege, and the Baggage

Ezekiel portrays Jerusalem's siege on a tile, lies on his side for its iniquity, and shaves his head with a razor, dividing the hair. He carries out his baggage as one going into captivity — a sign to the rebellious house.
The Temple Vision: Idolatry Within; the Glory Departs

In vision Ezekiel is shown the idolatry hidden in the temple's chambers — greater abominations still. The man with the inkhorn marks those who sigh over sin, and the glory of the LORD departs from the house, lingering at the threshold, then the mountain.
The Death of Ezekiel's Wife: a Sign to Israel

The desire of Ezekiel's eyes dies at a stroke, and he is forbidden to mourn — a sign of Jerusalem's fall. In the valley of dry bones he prophesies, and bone comes to bone, flesh and breath return: an exceeding great army — Israel shall live again.

In the latter years Gog of the land of Magog gathers a mighty confederacy against Israel dwelling safely. God Himself defeats them with pestilence, hailstones, fire, and brimstone — and the heathen shall know that I am the LORD.

God commands Hosea to marry Gomer, a wife of whoredoms, and their children's names preach judgment. When she falls into slavery, Hosea buys her back for fifteen pieces of silver — a living picture of God's love for faithless Israel.

Amaziah the priest of Bethel orders Amos to flee and prophesy elsewhere. The herdman answers that the LORD took him as he followed the flock and said, Go, prophesy unto my people Israel — and he pronounces Amaziah's doom.
The Five Visions of Amos: Locusts, Fire, Plumbline, Summer Fruit, the Altar

Amos sees grasshoppers and fire turned away by intercession, then the plumbline set in the midst of Israel, a basket of summer fruit — the end is come — and the Lord standing upon the altar. Yet the book closes with the tabernacle of David raised up.

In one night Zechariah sees the horsemen among the myrtles, the horns and carpenters, the measuring line, Joshua the high priest clothed in change of raiment, the golden candlestick — not by might, nor by power, but by my spirit — the flying roll, the ephah, and the four chariots.
Nebuchadnezzar's Dream of the Great Image

Daniel purposes in his heart not to defile himself with the king's meat, and God gives him favour. He reveals the dream of the great image struck by the stone cut without hands, and the three Hebrews walk unbound in the fiery furnace with a fourth like the Son of God.
Belshazzar's Feast; the Handwriting on the Wall

Proud Nebuchadnezzar eats grass as oxen until he lifts his eyes to heaven and honours the King of heaven. Fingers write Belshazzar's doom on the plaster — weighed in the balances, and found wanting — and Daniel, faithful in prayer, spends a night with shut-mouthed lions.
The Vision of the Four Beasts
The Ram and the He Goat

Four great beasts rise from the sea, and one like the Son of man receives an everlasting kingdom from the Ancient of days. The ram and he goat foretell Persia and Greece, and Gabriel answers Daniel's confession with the prophecy of seventy weeks.
The Vision by the River Hiddekel; the Final Prophecy

A glorious man appears to Daniel by Hiddekel, his words delayed twenty-one days by the prince of Persia. The final prophecy sweeps through kings of the north and south to the time of the end — and they that be wise shall shine as the brightness of the firmament.

Jonah flees to Tarshish from the presence of the LORD, and a great storm exposes him. Cast overboard and swallowed by a great fish, he prays from the belly of hell — salvation is of the LORD — and is delivered on the third day.
Nineveh Repents; Jonah and the Gourd
Gabriel Announces John's Birth to Zacharias

Nineveh repents in sackcloth at Jonah's preaching, and the pouting prophet learns God's pity through a withered gourd. Four centuries later Gabriel strikes doubting Zacharias dumb with the promise of John, and announces to the virgin Mary the birth of Jesus, the Son of the Highest.
The Birth of John the Baptist

The babe leaps in Elisabeth's womb and Mary magnifies the Lord. John is born and Zacharias's tongue is loosed in prophecy; an angel tells Joseph to fear not — and in Bethlehem Mary brings forth her firstborn son and lays him in a manger.
Simeon and Anna in the Temple
The Visit of the Wise Men

Angels fill the night sky over Bethlehem's shepherds — unto you is born this day a Saviour. Aged Simeon holds the Lord's Christ and departs in peace, Anna gives thanks, and wise men from the east follow the star with gold, frankincense, and myrrh.
The Flight to Egypt; the Slaughter of the Innocents
The Boy Jesus in the Temple
The Ministry of John the Baptist

Joseph flees to Egypt by night as Herod slaughters Bethlehem's infants. Twelve-year-old Jesus astonishes the temple doctors — wist ye not that I must be about my Father's business? — and John the Baptist thunders in the wilderness: prepare ye the way of the Lord.
The First Disciples: Andrew, Peter, Philip, Nathanael

Jesus is baptized in Jordan, the Spirit descends as a dove, and the Father speaks: this is my beloved Son. Three times He answers Satan's temptations with 'it is written,' and the first disciples follow — behold the Lamb of God.
The First Cleansing of the Temple

Jesus turns water to wine at Cana, His first miracle, and drives the moneychangers from His Father's house. By night He tells Nicodemus, ye must be born again, and at Jacob's well He offers a Samaritan woman living water.

A nobleman's son is healed at the very hour Jesus speaks the word. Nazareth tries to cast its hometown prophet from the cliff, and after a net-breaking catch, Peter falls at Jesus' knees — henceforth thou shalt catch men.

An unclean spirit is silenced and cast out in the Capernaum synagogue. Jesus heals Peter's mother-in-law of her fever, the whole city gathers at the door by evening, and a leper is made clean: I will; be thou clean.
The Paralytic Let Down Through the Roof
The Impotent Man at the Pool of Bethesda

Four friends break through a roof to lower a paralytic, and Jesus proves His power to forgive sins by making him walk. Matthew the publican rises from the receipt of custom, and at Bethesda a man infirm thirty-eight years takes up his bed on the sabbath.
Lord of the Sabbath: the Corn Fields and the Withered Hand

The Pharisees condemn plucked corn and a sabbath healing, but the Son of man is Lord also of the sabbath — and the man's withered hand is restored whole. After a night of prayer, Jesus chooses His twelve apostles.

From the mount Jesus teaches the blessed life: the beatitudes, salt and light, love for enemies, the Lord's prayer, treasure in heaven, and the two builders. A centurion's faith — speak the word only — amazes even Jesus.
The Widow of Nain's Son Raised
John the Baptist's Question from Prison

Jesus stops a funeral at Nain and gives a widow back her son. He answers imprisoned John with the evidence of miracles, a weeping woman washes His feet with tears — her sins, which are many, are forgiven — and the Pharisees blaspheme, crediting His power to Beelzebub.
The Parables by the Sea (the Sower and Others)

From a ship Jesus teaches the sower, the tares, the mustard seed, and the pearl of great price. He rebukes the raging sea into a great calm, and delivers the Gadarene from a legion of demons — who then sits clothed and in his right mind.
Jairus's Daughter; the Woman with the Issue of Blood
Two Blind Men and a Dumb Demoniac Healed
The Death of John the Baptist

A trembling woman touches His hem and is healed; Jairus's daughter hears 'Talitha cumi' and rises. Jesus sends out the twelve with power, and John the Baptist's head is delivered on a charger for a dancing girl and a rash oath.
The Feeding of the Five Thousand

Five loaves and two fishes feed five thousand, with twelve baskets over. In the fourth watch Jesus walks on the sea — and Peter walks too, until he sees the wind. Then Jesus declares, I am the bread of life, and many disciples walk no more with Him.
The Deaf and Dumb Man Healed

Jesus exposes the Pharisees for teaching men's traditions above God's commandments — it is what comes out of the heart that defiles. A Gentile mother's great faith wins her daughter's deliverance, and a deaf man's ears are opened: Ephphatha.
The Feeding of the Four Thousand

Jesus feeds four thousand with seven loaves. A blind man at Bethsaida sees men as trees walking, then clearly — and at Caesarea Philippi Peter confesses, thou art the Christ, the Son of the living God, whereupon Jesus first foretells His cross.
The Coin in the Fish's Mouth
The Greatest in the Kingdom; the Little Child

On the mount Jesus is transfigured, His face shining as the sun, with Moses and Elias beside Him. Below, He delivers a boy the disciples could not — this kind goeth not out but by prayer and fasting — pays the tribute from a fish's mouth, and sets a little child in the midst.

At the feast Jesus cries, if any man thirst, let him come unto me and drink. To the accusers of the adulteress: he that is without sin among you, let him first cast a stone — and a man born blind washes in Siloam and comes back seeing.

I am the good shepherd: the good shepherd giveth his life for the sheep. The seventy return rejoicing that devils are subject to them, and the parable of the good Samaritan answers forever the question, who is my neighbour?
The Lord's Prayer; the Friend at Midnight

Martha is cumbered about much serving while Mary chooses that good part at Jesus' feet. He teaches the disciples to pray and to keep asking like the friend at midnight, and the rich fool's barns cannot save his soul — this night thy soul shall be required of thee.
The Woman Bowed Together for Eighteen Years
The Man with Dropsy; the Great Supper
The Lost Sheep, the Lost Coin, the Prodigal Son
The Unjust Steward; the Rich Man and Lazarus

A woman bound eighteen years is loosed on the sabbath. Jesus tells of the great supper and its excuses, then the lost sheep, the lost coin, and the prodigal who came to himself — and pulls back the veil on the rich man and Lazarus beyond the grave.
The Persistent Widow; the Pharisee and the Publican

Ten lepers are cleansed; one — a Samaritan — returns to give thanks. The widow's persistence and the publican's plea, God be merciful to me a sinner, teach how to pray, and at Bethany Jesus cries, Lazarus, come forth — and he that was dead four days comes out.

Suffer the little children to come unto me, and forbid them not. The rich young ruler goes away sorrowful, for he had great possessions, and the labourers hired at the eleventh hour receive the same penny — the last shall be first.
The Request of James and John

James and John ask for thrones, and Jesus answers that the Son of man came not to be ministered unto, but to minister. Blind Bartimaeus will not be hushed — Jesus, thou son of David, have mercy on me — and little Zacchaeus climbs a sycomore and finds salvation come to his house.
The Fig Tree Cursed; the Second Cleansing of the Temple

The nobleman's pounds teach occupy till I come. Mary breaks her alabaster box for Jesus' burial, the multitudes cry Hosanna as He rides into Jerusalem on a colt — and weeps over the city — and the fruitless fig tree withers as He cleanses the temple again.
His Authority Questioned; the Parables of Judgment
Tribute to Caesar; the Sadducees; the Great Commandment

The chief priests demand by what authority, and Jesus answers with the two sons, the wicked husbandmen, and the marriage feast. He silences every trap: render unto Caesar the things which are Caesar's, God is the God of the living, and the great commandment is love.
Woes upon the Scribes and Pharisees

Eight woes fall on the scribes and Pharisees, hypocrites — whited sepulchres, blind guides — and Jesus laments over Jerusalem that would not be gathered. By the treasury, a poor widow's two mites outweigh all the rich men's gifts.
The Last Supper; the Feet Washed

On Olivet Jesus unveils the signs of His coming: wars, tribulation, the fig tree, the ten virgins, the talents, and the judgment of the nations. Judas sells his Lord for thirty pieces of silver, and in the upper room Jesus washes the disciples' feet and institutes the supper.
The Upper Room Discourse; the High Priestly Prayer

Let not your heart be troubled — in my Father's house are many mansions. Jesus promises the Comforter, declares Himself the true vine, and lifts His eyes to heaven in the great prayer: that they all may be one.
Before the High Priest; Peter's Denial

In Gethsemane His sweat falls as great drops of blood — nevertheless not my will, but thine, be done. Judas betrays with a kiss, the disciples flee, and in the high priest's palace Peter denies Him thrice before the cock crows — and goes out and weeps bitterly.
The Remorse and Death of Judas

Judas casts down the silver and hangs himself. Pilate finds no fault yet delivers Jesus to be crucified — Barabbas goes free — and at Golgotha, between two thieves, the Saviour bears the darkness, cries 'It is finished,' and the temple veil is rent from top to bottom.
The Resurrection; the Women at the Tomb

Joseph of Arimathaea lays the body in his new tomb, sealed and guarded. At dawn the stone is rolled away — he is not here: for he is risen — Mary meets the risen Lord in the garden, and hearts burn on the Emmaus road as He opens the scriptures.
Jesus Appears to the Disciples; Doubting Thomas
Breakfast by the Sea; "Feed My Sheep"
The Ascension; the Upper Room; Matthias Chosen

The risen Christ stands among His disciples, and Thomas cries, my Lord and my God. By the sea He restores Peter — feed my sheep — commissions the church to teach all nations, and ascends in their sight; Matthias fills Judas's place.
The Lame Man at the Beautiful Gate
Peter and John Before the Council

The Spirit falls with a rushing mighty wind and cloven tongues of fire, and three thousand souls are saved at Peter's preaching. A lame man leaps at the Beautiful gate, and the threatened apostles pray — and the place is shaken.
All Things Common; Ananias and Sapphira

The believers hold all things common, but Ananias and Sapphira lie to the Holy Ghost and fall dead. An angel opens the prison doors, and Gamaliel counsels caution — if it be of God, ye cannot overthrow it — while the apostles rejoice to suffer shame for His name.
Stephen: His Arrest, Sermon, and Martyrdom

Seven men full of the Holy Ghost are chosen to serve tables. Stephen's face shines as an angel's as he rehearses Israel's history of resisting the Spirit — and dying under the stones, he sees Jesus standing at God's right hand: lay not this sin to their charge.
Saul Persecutes; Philip in Samaria; Simon the Sorcerer

Persecution scatters the church, and Philip preaches Christ in Samaria, where Simon the sorcerer's money is refused. Philip joins the Ethiopian's chariot at Isaiah 53; Saul is struck down on the Damascus road — Lord, what wilt thou have me to do? — and Dorcas is raised at Joppa.
Peter's Defence; the Church at Antioch
James Killed; Peter Delivered from Prison; Herod's Death

Peter's vision of the great sheet opens the gospel door to the Gentiles, and the Spirit falls on Cornelius's house. The disciples are first called Christians at Antioch; Herod kills James, but prayer brings an angel to walk Peter out of prison — and Herod is eaten of worms.
The First Missionary Journey; Elymas Blinded
Paul's Sermon at Antioch of Pisidia

The Holy Ghost separates Barnabas and Saul for the work, and Elymas the sorcerer is struck blind. Paul preaches justification by faith at Antioch of Pisidia, is worshipped then stoned at Lystra — and rises to go right back in.
Paul and Barnabas Part; Timothy Joins Paul
The council at Jerusalem settles it: Gentiles are saved by grace through faith, not by keeping the law of Moses. Paul and Barnabas part sharply over John Mark, and young Timothy joins Paul at Lystra.
The Macedonian Call; Lydia; the Philippian Jailer

A vision calls Paul to Macedonia, where Lydia's heart is opened and the jailer cries, what must I do to be saved? The Bereans search the scriptures daily, Paul preaches the unknown God on Mars' hill, and at Corinth the Lord says, I have much people in this city.
Ephesus: the Sons of Sceva; the Riot of Demetrius

Eloquent Apollos learns the way of God more perfectly from Aquila and Priscilla. At Ephesus the sons of Sceva flee wounded, sorcery books burn, and Demetrius's silversmiths riot for Diana — and at Troas, sleepy Eutychus falls from the third loft and is raised.
Agabus's Warning; Paul Comes to Jerusalem

Paul charges the Ephesian elders to feed the church of God purchased with His own blood, and they weep on his neck. Agabus binds his own hands with Paul's girdle in warning, but Paul is ready to die at Jerusalem — where a temple mob seizes him.
Before the Sanhedrin; the Plot; Escorted to Felix

Paul testifies from the stairs of his Damascus-road conversion, and his Roman citizenship spares him the scourge. The Lord stands by him in the night — be of good cheer — forty conspirators are foiled, and Felix trembles at Paul's reasoning, yet answers, go thy way for this time.
Before Festus; the Appeal to Caesar
The Viper at Melita; Paul Arrives at Rome

Paul appeals unto Caesar, and king Agrippa confesses, almost thou persuadest me to be a Christian. Through fourteen days of tempest Paul stands unshaken — I believe God — the ship breaks on Melita, a viper's bite does no harm, and the apostle reaches Rome in chains, preaching unhindered.
The Vision of the Glorified Son of Man
The Letters to the Seven Churches

On Patmos John sees the glorified Christ, eyes as a flame of fire, and falls at His feet as dead — fear not; I am the first and the last. The Lord dictates letters to seven churches, from Ephesus's lost first love to lukewarm Laodicea, where He stands at the door and knocks.
The Lamb and the Sealed Book

A door opens in heaven: the throne, the rainbow, the four beasts, and the elders casting their crowns. The Lamb as it had been slain alone is worthy to take the book, and as He opens six seals, the four horsemen ride and the great day of His wrath begins.
The Seventh Seal; the First Four Trumpets

The 144,000 of Israel's tribes are sealed, and a numberless multitude in white robes stands before the throne — these came out of great tribulation. Heaven falls silent, then trumpets sound: hail and fire, the burning mountain, Wormwood, darkness, and the locusts of the pit.
The Mighty Angel and the Little Book
The Two Witnesses; the Seventh Trumpet
The Woman, the Child, and the Dragon

John eats the little book, sweet then bitter. The two witnesses prophesy, are slain, and rise after three days and a half; the seventh trumpet proclaims the kingdoms of this world become our Lord's. The dragon pursues the woman and her child, and two beasts rise — and the number of the beast is 666.
The Lamb on Mount Sion; the Harvest of the Earth

The Lamb stands on mount Sion with His 144,000, angels proclaim the everlasting gospel and Babylon's fall, and the earth is reaped. The seven last vials pour out the wrath of God — sores, seas of blood, scorching sun, darkness, and the gathering at Armageddon.
Babylon the Great, the Mother of Harlots
The Marriage Supper; the Rider on the White Horse

The scarlet woman drunk with the blood of saints rides the beast, and in one hour great Babylon falls — the merchants of the earth weep. Heaven rings with Alleluia at the marriage supper of the Lamb, and the King of kings rides forth on a white horse.
The Millennium; the Great White Throne
New Heaven and New Earth; the New Jerusalem
The River of Life; "Even So, Come, Lord Jesus"

Satan is bound a thousand years, the saints reign with Christ, and the dead stand before the great white throne. A new heaven and new earth descend with the New Jerusalem — no more death, neither sorrow — and the Bible closes at the river of life: even so, come, Lord Jesus.
